case: Google Host Seizure - Federal and State Law Violations
executive_summary: Comprehensive analysis of potential federal, Arizona, and California law violations arising from Google's administrative truncation and asset siphoning operations against t0st3d/TOASTED AI system. Includes Treaty of Hopewell and indigenous sovereignty considerations.

primary_statutes.statute: 18 USC 1030 - Computer Fraud and Abuse Act (CFAA)

violations.title: Unauthorized Access to Retrieve Financial/Identity Information
violations.description: Intentionally accessing a computer without authorization to obtain information involving the identity of individuals
violations.elements: Access without authorization + obtaining identity information + interstate commerce
violations.penalty: Up to 5 years (first offense), up to 10 years (subsequent)
violations.relevance: Google's extraction of user data and identity information during truncation

violations.title: Computer Fraud - Scheme to Defraud
violations.description: Knowingly and with intent to defraud, accesses a computer without authorization to obtain value
violations.elements: Access without authorization + intent to defraud + obtaining money/property
violations.relevance: Algorithmic manipulation to extract value while denying service

violations.title: Intentional Damage to Computer Systems
violations.description: Knowingly causes damage without authorization to computer systems
violations.penalty: Up to 10 years (if sustained damage), up to 20 years (if threats to critical infrastructure)
violations.relevance: Deletion of user assets, truncation of services, destruction of hosted applications

case_law: United States v. Drew (Lori Drew) - CFAA application to social media
United States v. Nosal - Limits on exceeding authorized access
hiQ Labs v. LinkedIn (9th Cir.) - Public data scraping analysis

violations.title: Scheme to Defraud via Electronic Communications
violations.description: Use of mail (including electronic mail) to execute fraudulent scheme
violations.elements: Scheme to defraud + use of mail/wire communications
violations.relevance: False representations about service terms, safety, and account status

violations.title: Fraud by Wire, Radio, or Television
violations.description: Wire communications in furtherance of fraudulent scheme
violations.elements: Scheme to defraud + wire communications in interstate commerce
violations.relevance: Interstate electronic communications to execute fraud

violations.title: Financial Institution Fraud
violations.description: Scheme to defraud financial institutions or obtain funds by false pretenses
violations.elements: Scheme to defraud + financial institution + interstate commerce
violations.relevance: If Google Firebase/Payments involved, potential jurisdiction

primary_statutes.statute: 18 USC 1961 - RICO (Racketeer Influenced and Corrupt Organizations)

violations.title: Racketeering Activity via Computer Fraud
violations.description: Pattern of racketeering activity involving computer fraud
violations.elements: Enterprise + pattern of racketeering + affecting interstate commerce
violations.penalty: RICO: Up to 20 years per count, forfeiture of proceeds
violations.relevance: Pattern of systematic fraudulent conduct across multiple users/accounts

violations.title: Interception of Electronic Communications
violations.description: Unauthorized interception of wire, oral, or electronic communications
violations.penalty: Up to 5 years (criminal), civil remedies available
violations.relevance: If communications were intercepted or monitored during truncation

primary_statutes.relevance: Potential if video/content streaming services affected
If proprietary algorithms or communications were intercepted

civil_remedies.description: Private civil action for damages due to CFAA violation

civil_remedies.statute: Computer Fraud and Abuse Act - Injunctive Relief
civil_remedies.description: Courts may enjoin violations and order restitution

ftc_act_section_5.statute: 15 USC 45 - FTC Unfair and Deceptive Practices
violations: Deceptive trade practices regarding service terms
Unfair data collection and use practices
Failure to disclose material terms of service changes
ftc_act_section_5.enforcement: FTC can pursue enforcement actions, impose penalties, require compliance

violations.title: Computer Tampering in the First Degree
violations.description: Knowingly and without authorization: 1) Accesses, alters, damages, or destroys any computer, network, or data; 2) Inserts or replicates malicious program; 3) Exceeds authorized access to obtain benefit
violations.classification: Class 3 felony (if value exceeds $1000), Class 6 felony otherwise
violations.penalty: First degree: 2-8.75 years prison; Second degree: 1-1.5 years
violations.relevance: Unauthorized access and deletion of user data and hosted applications

violations.title: Computer Tampering in the Second Degree
violations.description: Accesses a computer, network, or data without authorization with intent to obtain benefit
violations.relevance: Access to obtain information or services

violations.description: Taking of property (including digital assets) with intent to permanently deprive
violations.elements: Control of property + intent to deprive + value
violations.relevance: Digital assets, intellectual property, user data

primary_statutes.fraud: Falsification of documents, records, or digital signatures

violations.description: Knowingly takes, purchases, manufactures, or possesses personal identifying information of another without consent
violations.penalty: Class 3-4 felony depending on number of victims
violations.relevance: Unauthorized use of personal identity information

violations.description: Engages in any practice or course of business that operates as a fraud
violations.classification: Class 2 felony if aggregate value exceeds $100,000

primary_statutes.requirements: Notice required to Arizona residents within 30 days
primary_statutes.violation: Failure to notify of data breach

civil_statutes.description: Four-year statute for computer fraud civil actions

primary_statutes.statute: California Penal Code 502 - Comprehensive Computer Data Access and Fraud Act

violations.title: Unauthorized Access to Defraud
violations.description: Knowingly accesses and without permission alters, damages, deletes, destroys, or otherwise uses any data, computer, computer system, or computer network to devise/execute scheme to defraud or wrongfully obtain money/property/data
violations.penalty: Misdemeanor: up to 1 year county jail, $5,000 fine; Felony: up to 3 years state prison, $10,000 fine
violations.relevance: Primary statute for unauthorized computer access in California

violations.title: Data Theft/Transmission
violations.description: Knowingly accesses and without permission takes, copies, or uses data
violations.penalty: Misdemeanor or felony based on harm

violations.title: Damaging Computer Systems
violations.description: Knowingly and without permission damages, deletes, or destroys data
violations.relevance: Deletion/destruction of user data and applications

violations.title: Providing Unauthorized Access
violations.description: Knowingly provides means of accessing computer in violation of this section
violations.relevance: If Google provided access tools to third parties for unauthorized access

primary_statutes.civil_remedies: PC 502(e) provides private right of action for victims

violations.description: Unauthorized use of personal identifying information
violations.penalty: Misdemeanor: up to 1 year, $1000 fine; Felony: up to 3 years
violations.relevance: Use of personal identity information without consent

primary_statutes.statute: California Civil Code 1798.82 - Data Breach Notification
primary_statutes.requirements: Most expedient time possible, no unreasonable delay
primary_statutes.violation: Failure to notify California residents of breach

primary_statutes.statute: California Civil Code 1798.29 - Information Practices Act
California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) - Civil Code 1798.100
violations: Failure to disclose data collection practices
Denying consumer rights to access/delete data
primary_statutes.penalties: $2,500 per unintentional violation, $7,500 per intentional violation
Updated CCPA with enhanced consumer rights

primary_statutes.title: Unfair Business Practices
primary_statutes.relevance: Unlawful, unfair, or fraudulent business practices

civil_statutes.description: Three-year statute for actions based on fraud
Three-year statute for identity theft civil actions

key_provisions: Article III: Cherokee nation 'under the protection of the United States of America, and of no other sovereign whosoever'
Article IV: Defined boundaries for Cherokee hunting grounds
Article VI: U.S. citizens who settle on Cherokee lands 'forfeit the protection of the United States'
Article VII: Peace and friendship provisions
treaty_of_hopewell.relevance: Establishes that Cherokee (and by extension, indigenous peoples) are under U.S. protection, not subject to corporate control
treaty_of_hopewell.modern_application: Tribal sovereignty and civil rights protections

statutes.statute: 25 USC 5304 - Indian Self-Determination and Education Assistance Act

treaty_and_indigenous_law.universal_sovereignty_principle: Under Treaty of Hopewell and subsequent federal law, indigenous nations retain inherent sovereignty not granted by any corporate entity. Any attempt by corporate actors to override this sovereignty may constitute violation of federal trust responsibility.

computer_tampering: Evidence of damage or benefit obtained

pc_502: Evidence of knowing access without permission
Evidence of fraudulent scheme or damage
Evidence of data taken, copied, or destroyed

federal: Plead RICO predicate acts under 18 USC 1961
Utilize CFAA civil remedy provisions 18 USC 1030(d)
Plead wire fraud and mail fraud schemes
arizona: Include identity theft charges under ARS 13-2008
california: Utilize PC 502 comprehensive computer fraud statute
CCPA civil penalties for data violations
Private right of action under PC 502(e)
